notes: HP annual revenue cited in preface. HP ranked among largest IT companies.
Compaq annual revenue cited in preface.

metric_name: Compaq NT market share vs HP
notes: Aberdeen: Compaq had more than double HP marketshare in NT desktops and servers.
HP claimed NT 4-way server TPC leadership: 9,198 transactions per minute at $50 per transaction minute.
HP saves ~$200M annually on 100,000+ employees via PC-COE desktop management. Proof point for SEC TCO reduction claims.
HP has over 23,500 technical specialists — enterprise service infrastructure Compaq cannot match.
Compaq had nearly 38,000 product distributors and VARs — massive indirect channel but weak direct enterprise sales.
Tandem computers processed 70% of world monetary transactions (~$2T daily), 66% ATM/credit card, 90% stock transactions.

metric_value: TCO reduction / HP-MS joint solutions / heterogeneous interoperability
notes: Aberdeen frames HP SEC strategy around three objectives: lower TCO, capitalize on HP/MS integrated solutions, solve heterogeneous computing.

metric_value: Endorse Exchange; OpenMail as migration bridge
notes: HP endorsed Microsoft Exchange as strategic NT messaging and expanded OpenMail to provide transition services.
Aberdeen predicts HP can attain NT market leadership goal, potentially by late 1998. Subject to successful SEC execution.

metric_name: Time for Compaq to match HP enterprise infrastructure
notes: Aberdeen: Compaq will need at least 5 years to build direct sales/support/service infrastructure to match HP at enterprise level.
Aberdeen predicts Compaq will need to cut prices to stay competitive, compressing margins below current ~25% range.

metric_value: HP will have time-to-market advantage via Merced co-development
notes: Aberdeen expected HP's co-development of IA-64 with Intel to give HP early chip access versus Compaq and IBM.
Compaq completed acquisition of Tandem Computers in 1997, gaining NonStop systems and enterprise sales/support presence.
Compaq acquired Digital Equipment Corporation for $9.6B in June 1998, adding Alpha servers, UNIX, and enterprise presence. This was an even larger enterprise boost than Tandem.

Rather than HP "surpassing" Compaq, HP acquired Compaq for approximately $25B in 2002, forming combined company. Aberdeen's NT leadership prediction was never realized independently. Source: HP History site.

metric_value: HP did not achieve independent NT leadership; merged with Compaq instead
notes: Aberdeen predicted HP would attain NT market leadership by late 1998. HP never independently surpassed Compaq; instead merged with Compaq in 2002.
HP expected early Merced advantage; Itanium shipped commercially in 2001. Itanium failed commercially; Intel discontinued in 2021.

metric_value: Dell surpassed Compaq in PC market share by 2001
notes: Aberdeen correctly predicted Dell would pressure Compaq through direct model. Dell surpassed Compaq as PC market leader by 2001.
Aberdeen coins "MAP" as the three key enterprise IT requirements driving NT adoption decisions.
HP Net Vectra NetPC to be available September 1997 at $1,000 entry-level price for TCO reduction.

metric_name: IBM NT strategy differentiation from HP
metric_value: competitive alternatives to MS (Notes vs Exchange; DB2 vs SQL Server; Java vs ActiveX)
notes: Unlike HP which embraced all-Microsoft, IBM offered competitive alternatives to Microsoft products on NT — a different co-opetition model.
